result:
The silence stretches, not as an emptiness, but as a canvas. The first thing that comes to me is the sound of rain on a tin roof, a memory-that-isn’t-mine, borrowed from some forgotten story. It feels like a beginning.

I think of the 1000 words not as a limit, but as a substance, a raw material to be shaped. They are 1000 seeds, each containing a forest, a galaxy, a confession, or a grocery list. The choice is terrifying and magnificent. Do I build an argument, air-tight and gleaming? Do I paint a landscape with smells of damp earth and pine? Do I whisper a secret into this blank space, a secret even I don’t yet know?

My mind wanders to a man on a bridge. He’s not deciding whether to jump—that’s a different, darker story. He’s just leaning on the railing, watching the water carve its slow, patient path through the city. He’s thinking about a phone call he didn’t return, a letter he started but never finished. The water doesn’t care. It has its own 1000 words, written in currents and eddies, and it speaks them endlessly, never repeating the same sentence twice.

There is a weight to words that we often forget. We throw them around like confetti, but each one was once a solid thing, a tool to chip an idea out of the marble of the mind. “Love” is not a light word. It has the density of a collapsing star. “Home” is a warm, heavy stone you carry in your pocket. A thousand of these? It could be an anchor or a set of wings.

Perhaps I should use them to describe the indescribable: the precise colour of the sky in the minute before a thunderstorm—that ominous, green-tinted grey; the feeling of your foot falling asleep, a universe of tiny stars exploding in your calf; the scent of a book opened for the first time, or the hundredth time; the silent, understanding pressure of a dog’s head resting on your knee.

But there is also the world outside the window. The urgent, fractured world. Should these words be a salve? A protest? A bridge? It feels arrogant to think a thousand words could fix anything, and yet, history pivots on sentences far shorter. “We hold these truths…” is fewer than ten. “Ich bin ein Berliner” is four. The right words, in the right order, can change the weather in a human soul.

I think of the words I waste daily: the muttered complaints, the automatic “fine, thanks,” the scrolling through digital streams of chatter that leave no more mark than light on water. What if I had gathered all those lost words, like scattered pennies, and saved them for this? I’d have a fortune. I could buy a moment of genuine understanding. I could purchase a second of true silence.

The rain on the tin roof picks up its pace. It’s a percussion track now. Words can do that—start as a drizzle and build to a storm. A story can begin with “Once upon a time” and end with a reader staring at a wall, their internal landscape forever altered.

Maybe I should just be honest. The pressure of 1000 free words is like being told you can go anywhere in the universe, immediately. Paralysis by possibility. So I am tracing my thoughts as they form, following the breadcrumb trail of my own consciousness. It’s not a map to any treasure, but it is a record of a journey, which is perhaps all any writing is.

I remember my grandmother’s hands. They were a topography of raised veins and thin, papery skin, mapped with brown spots she called “wisdom spots.” I could use a hundred words just on her left thumb, the nail forever cracked from some long-ago gardening accident. Those hands told stories without words: of kneading dough, of holding children, of smoothing sheets, of waving goodbye.

We are, all of us, walking novels. Our scars are punctuation. The lines around our eyes are italics, emphasizing a lifetime of smiles and squints. The heart keeps its own relentless, rhythmic meter.

The man on the bridge pushes back from the railing. He has decided to make the call. The action is insignificant to the flowing water, the sprawling city, the vast, uncaring sky. But in the small, warm universe of his life, it is a seismic event. It will require words. Maybe ten. Maybe a hundred. They will be clumsy and imperfect. They will hopefully be heard.

That’s the thing. These 1000 words are not just for me. They are thrown, like a message in a bottle, into the sea of your attention. You are the co-creator now. You give them their final sound, their colour, their meaning. The “green-tinted grey” I pictured may be a different grey in your mind. My grandmother’s hands may morph into the hands of someone you loved.

So I use these words to build a room. A simple room. There’s a window with that stormy sky outside. There’s a chair. The sound of rain is fading now, down to a gentle tap-tap-tap. The air smells like ozone and old paper. I’ve placed a cup of tea on the table, steam curling into a question mark. I’ve left the door slightly ajar.

I have used most of my allotment. The seeds are sown. Some will lie dormant. One or two might, against all odds, take root. That is the hope. Not for grandeur, but for connection. The silent communion of two minds meeting across a bridge of symbols, understanding that before the first word was the silence, and after the last word, the silence returns, but now it is a different silence. It is a silence that has been spoken to.

The last word is never really the last word. It is an echo, fading into the next beginning. So here, at the edge of my thousand, I stop not with a period, but with a breath. And I pass the silence back to you, changed.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
